behaviors.description: Only files changed in the pull request are analyzed; unchanged files in the repository are not scanned
behaviors.justification: The failure is integration-level wiring: the code must call the GitHub API, filter the returned changed files, and only analyze those. A pure Unit test of filter logic wouldn't catch if the code accidentally scans an entire directory tree instead. A mock GitHub API response paired with repo file listing and verification that only the intersection is analyzed catches the real failure mode without requiring a real API call.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the GitHub API to return a specific subset of changed files (e.g., 5 files). Mock the repo file scanner to return a larger superset (e.g., 50 files). Run the lint step and verify that (a) only the 5 changed files are analyzed, (b) analysis output contains no files outside that changed set.

behaviors.description: Findings are grouped by severity (error, warning, info) in the PR review comment
behaviors.justification: This is deterministic business logic with no I/O or external dependencies. The failure is a grouping logic bug: findings exist but are not grouped correctly, or groups are in the wrong order. A mock linter providing findings with mixed severity levels, passed through the grouping function, with assertion on output structure catches the failure at Unit level.
behaviors.test_description: Create a list of mock findings with mixed severity levels (5 errors, 3 warnings, 2 info). Call the grouping function. Assert output contains three sections, each containing the correct findings, in the expected order (errors first, then warnings, then info).

behaviors.description: Linting pipeline completes analysis of 200 files within the 10-minute GitHub Actions timeout
behaviors.justification: Performance failure depends on real subprocess execution and actual file I/O overhead. Mocking the linter to be instant would hide slowness in the real linting process and loop overhead. The failure would not be caught by an Integration test that stubs out the slow operation. Real subprocess calls, real file I/O, and actual elapsed time measurement are required to reveal algorithmic or subprocess inefficiencies.
behaviors.test_description: Generate or copy 200 representative source files (mix of languages). Run the full linting step with real subprocess invocations (not mocked). Measure elapsed time from start to finish. Assert that completion time is less than 10 minutes. Optionally, profile subprocess spawn overhead and I/O concurrency.

behaviors.description: When the linter process crashes on a malformed file, the remaining files are still analyzed and results are posted
behaviors.justification: The failure is in exception handling and loop control at the integration boundary between subprocess management and file iteration. A mocked subprocess that raises an exception at a specific file, paired with verification that the loop continues and remaining files are processed, catches the logic failure. Subprocess edge cases (broken pipes, SIGKILL) would require System level, but the primary failure—missing exception handling—is caught at Integration.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the linter subprocess to raise an exception when analyzing file 50 of 100. Run the lint step. Assert that (a) files 1–49 are analyzed successfully, (b) file 50 raises an error that is logged, (c) files 51–100 are still analyzed, (d) final results are posted containing only files 1–49 and 51–100 with no error for file 50.

behaviors.description: Duplicate findings on the same line from overlapping rules are deduplicated in the output
behaviors.justification: Deduplication is pure logic: given a list of finding objects, remove entries with identical (line, rule, severity) tuples. No I/O, no subprocess, no external API. A mock linter providing duplicate findings, passed through the deduplication function, with assertion on uniqueness catches the failure at Unit level.
behaviors.test_description: Create a list of finding objects where two rules (eslint and prettier) both flag line 42 with the same severity (info). Pass this list to the deduplication function. Assert that the output contains exactly one entry for line 42, not two.
